content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 15, 2010

Jennifer K. McCain
Assistant City Attorney
City of Escondido
201 North Broadway
Escondido, California 92026

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-177

Dear Ms. McCain:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Escondido City Mayor Sam
Adbed regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and
is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does
not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the
applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest or
Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

1. May Mayor Abed participate in the city council and/or Community Development
Commission's consideration of a governmental decision to acquire land and construct a minor
league ballpark?

CONCLUSION

1. Mayor Abed is presumed not to have a conflict of interest in the governmental
decision because his real property is located more than 500 feet from the property that is the
subject of the governmental decision. However, this presumption may be rebutted if there are
factors that indicate there will be a material financial effect on Mayor Abed's property, as
discussed below.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-177
Page No. 2

FACTS

Mayor Abed owns real property located at 540 and 562 W. Grand Avenue. Mayor Abed
receives rental income from the property located at 562 W. Grand Avenue. He leases the
property to Tom and Judy Fitzgerald. Tom and Judy Fitzgerald own CrossFit Xystum LLC² and
have two years remaining on their lease. Tom and Judy Fitzgerald pay $3,100 per month to
Mayor Abed. The property at 540 W. Grand is vacant.

The governmental decision facing the Escondido City Council involves acquiring land
and constructing a minor league ballpark. Specifically, the City will be acquiring two parcels of
land and using a portion of its own property to construct a minor league ballpark that will be
leased to a minor league baseball team. The parcels of property are adjacent to each other and
north of the "Spruce Street property," located at 480 North Spruce Street consisting of 3.78
acres; the North County Transit District property, consisting of 1.68 acres; and the existing City
public works yard consisting of 7.82 acres. The ballpark will be an open-air, natural grass, state-
of-the-art Minor League Baseball facility with a seating capacity of approximately 9,000. It is
anticipated that the project will be developed by the Escondido Community Development
Commission as a redevelopment project. Councilmembers also serve as member of the
Community Development Commission.

City staff has prepared a map showing the location of Mayor Abed's properties in
relation to the "Spruce Street Property" as described. It appears from the map that Mayor
Abed's properties located at 540 and 562 W. Grand Avenue are located well over 500 feet from
the "Spruce Street Property," at approximately 1,250 feet and 1,270 feet, respectively.

The city council has not yet made any formal decisions regarding acquiring the land and
constructing the ballpark. Mayor Abed seeks advice regarding whether he may participate in the
various decisions to acquire the land, constructing and financing the park, as well as the various
land use entitlements that will be required for the project. Mayor Abed also seeks advice
regarding what procedures must be followed if he is legally required to abstain from participating
in any governmental decisions regarding the ballpark.

POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,

² CrossFit Xystum is a fitness training center (see www.crossfitxysum.com).

File No. A-10-177
Page No. 3

however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.

STEPS 1 & 2: IS MAYOR ABED A PUBLIC OFFICIAL MAKING, PARTICIPATING IN MAKING, OR
INFLUENCING A GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?

As a mayor/community development commission member under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, he
may not make, participate in making, or otherwise use his official position to influence any decisions that will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on any of his economic interests. Because Mayor Abed will
be called upon to consider proposed projects before the city council, he will be making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his official position to influence a governmental
decision.³

STEP 3: DOES MAYOR ABED HAVE A POTENTIALLY DISQUALIFYING ECONOMIC INTEREST?

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)

³ If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("87200 filers" include city councilmembers and
planning commission members), he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting,
then he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic
interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation
18702.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration
of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a
member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d)
apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. A-10-177
Page No. 4

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family — this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)

You have indicated that Mayor Abed has a real property economic interest in two
properties located at 540 and 562 W. Grand Avenue respectively.³ You have also provided
information regarding Mayor Abed's source-of-income economic interest from his tenants of the
one commercial rental property. Additionally, Mayor Abed has an economic interest in his
business entity that rents the property.

STEP 4: IS THE ECONOMIC INTEREST DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY INVOLVED IN THE
GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?⁵

"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a private person's economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly or indirectly involved in the governmental decision." (Regulation 18704(a).) For governmental decisions that affect real property interests, the
standards set forth in regulation 18704.2 apply. (Regulation 18704(a)(2).)

Real Property:

Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part:

"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is
directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of
that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property [that] is the subject of the governmental decision."

You have indicated that the two properties owned by Mayor Abed's are located 1,250 and
1,270 feet from the ball-park site. Therefore, because the site is more than 500 feet from the two
properties, Mayor Abed's real property is indirectly involved in the governmental decisions.

Source of Income and Business Entity

For governmental decisions that affect business entities and sources of income, the
standards set forth in regulation 18704.1 apply. (Regulation 18704(a)(1).)

³ The Statement of Economic Interests you provided indicates that the properties each have a value in
excess of $2,000.

⁵ If a public official's economic interest in not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered
"indirectly involved." (Regulation 18704(a).)

File No. A-10-177
Page No. 5

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, is
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or by an agent: (1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; (2) Is a named
party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit,
or contract with, the subject person.

"(b) If a business entity, source of income, or source of a gift is directly involved in a
governmental decision, apply the materiality standards in [Regulation 18705.1(b),
Regulation 18705.3(a), or Regulation 18705.4(a)], respectively. If a business entity,
source of income, or source of a gift is not directly involved in a governmental
decision, apply the materiality standards in [Regulation 18705.1(c), Regulation
18705.3(b), or Regulation 18705.4(b)], respectively."

Because neither the business entity nor the source of income to Mayor Abed will be
initiating the proceeding or a named party in the proceeding, both of these economic interests are
indirectly involved.

STEP 5: MATERIALITY STANDARD

A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).)

Real Property:

For indirectly involved real property, Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) provides that the financial
effect of a governmental decision on real property is presumed not to be material. "This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the
government decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in which the
official has an economic interest, which makes it reasonable foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect of the real property in which the public official has an interest."
Examples of circumstances that include decisions that:

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;

(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest; and

(C) The character of the neighborhood including… substantial effects on:
traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the neighborhood." (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(A-C).)

File No. A-10-177
Page No. 6

Generally, when a public official's property is located more than 500 feet from the
property that is the subject of the governmental decision, we will conclude that there will be no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the official's property. However, when the
property of the change brought about by the governmental decision is such that it is clear that
there may be some affects on properties beyond 500 feet, we must consider those affects.

A Triple-A Minor League Ballpark could bring changes that would certainly implicate
the circumstances, listed in Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(C), as well as others, that may be used to
rebut the presumption that there will be no material affect.6 There certainly will be some impact
because of more traffic in the area surrounding the park and the noise from the park. The impact
is lessened because the surrounding properties all appear to be commercial and industrial.
Furthermore, the impact from the park is likely true for a great many properties surrounding the
site, with some properties being affected to a greater degree than others.

When the Commission established the 500 foot rule it was intended to serve, as much as
possible, as a bright-line test for determining when the law will consider a financial effect on real
property to be material. Within 500 feet, even a one-penny effect is material, and the financial effect will not be considered material unless there are specific circumstances
under which the official property will receive a benefit or suffer a detriment substantially
disproportionate to other properties affected by the decision.

You have provided a map and other information regarding the site of the proposed
ballpark and its proximity to the Abed properties. It does not appear that the Abed properties
will be significantly impacted by ingress or egress to the proposed ballpark site. The properties
are commercial properties surrounded by other commercial and/or industrial properties and,
therefore, are unlikely to be impacted by noise or privacy issues or other changes to the "traits of
the neighborhood." In short, on the basis of information provided at this point in time, it does
not appear that the presumption of nonmateriality has been rebutted. Accordingly, with respect
to the real property economic interest, Mayor Abed does not have a conflict of interest
participating in the referenced decision.

Business Entities and Source of Income:

For business entities and sources of income that are business entities that are indirectly
involved the materiality standards are set forth in Regulation 18905.1(c). The dollar thresholds
for materiality vary according to the size of the business. You have not provided any
information regarding the revenues of either Mayor Abed business or that of his source of
income but assuming that they are both relatively small businesses (net yearly income of under
$750,000), the materiality standards are set forth in Regulation 18705.1 (c)(4) and state that the
financial effect of the governmental decision is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

6 Because the Commission has not provided a threshold for what is material with respect to indirectly
involved real property, we do not have any dollar threshold for deciding what is material.

File No. A-10-177
Page No. 7

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or

(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or

(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.

STEP 6: REASONABLY FORESEEABLE

Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good faith
effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-113; Moock Advice Letter, No. A-01-140; O'Harra
Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.)

STEPS SEVEN AND EIGHT: PUBLIC GENERALLY AND LEGALLY REQUIRED
PARTICIPATION

You have not given any facts indicating that either the "public generally" exception or
that the "legally required participation" exception would apply.

If you have any questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: William S. Dankeit
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

WJL:jgl

foreseeable at the time the decision is made depeids on the facts surroundinj ihe decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, iut it must be more

CONCLUSION: 1. Mayor Abed is presumed not to have a conflict of interest in the governmental

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 December 15, 2010 Jennifer K. McCain Assistant City Attorney City of Escondido 201 North Broadway Escondido, California 92026 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-177 Dear Ms. McCain: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Escondido City Mayor Sam Adbed regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION 1. May Mayor Abed participate in the city council and/or Community Development Commission's consideration of a governmental decision to acquire land and construct a minor league ballpark? CONCLUSION 1. Mayor Abed is presumed not to have a conflict of interest in the governmental decision because his real property is located more than 500 feet from the property that is the subject of the governmental decision. However, this presumption may be rebutted if there are factors that indicate there will be a material financial effect on Mayor Abed's property, as discussed below. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-10-177 Page No. 2 FACTS Mayor Abed owns real property located at 540 and 562 W. Grand Avenue. Mayor Abed receives rental income from the property located at 562 W. Grand Avenue. He leases the property to Tom and Judy Fitzgerald. Tom and Judy Fitzgerald own CrossFit Xystum LLC² and have two years remaining on their lease. Tom and Judy Fitzgerald pay $3,100 per month to Mayor Abed. The property at 540 W. Grand is vacant. The governmental decision facing the Escondido City Council involves acquiring land and constructing a minor league ballpark. Specifically, the City will be acquiring two parcels of land and using a portion of its own property to construct a minor league ballpark that will be leased to a minor league baseball team. The parcels of property are adjacent to each other and north of the "Spruce Street property," located at 480 North Spruce Street consisting of 3.78 acres; the North County Transit District property, consisting of 1.68 acres; and the existing City public works yard consisting of 7.82 acres. The ballpark will be an open-air, natural grass, state- of-the-art Minor League Baseball facility with a seating capacity
